topic: Economic Impact of British Rule — Drain of Wealth, Deindustrialisation & Famines

questions.text: Who coined the term 'Drain of Wealth' to describe the economic exploitation of India by Britain?
options: Bal Gangadhar Tilak
Gopal Krishna Gokhale
Dadabhai Naoroji
R.C. Dutt
questions.explanation: Dadabhai Naoroji (1825–1917) coined the term 'Drain of Wealth' (also called Economic Drain) to describe the one-way flow of money from India to Britain — for which India received no equivalent in return. He first articulated this in his 1867 paper 'England's Debt to India' and developed it fully in 'Poverty and Un-British Rule in India' (1901). He distinguished this from normal trade (where goods are exchanged for payment) — the 'drain' was extraction: India's wealth left and never came back, in the form of Home Charges, profit remittances, and uncompensated export surplus. Naoroji was also the first Indian elected to the British Parliament (1892, Finsbury, London).
questions.source: SSC CGL — Dadabhai Naoroji and 'Drain of Wealth' is one of the most consistently tested economic history facts

questions.text: The book 'Economic History of India' (1902) — which documented how British policy had systematically impoverished India — was written by:
options: Dadabhai Naoroji
Romesh Chandra Dutt (R.C. Dutt)
M.G. Ranade
G.K. Gokhale
questions.explanation: Romesh Chandra Dutt (R.C. Dutt) wrote 'Economic History of India' in two volumes (1902 and 1904). Using statistical data, he documented how British land revenue policy, trade policy, and industrial policy had systematically drained India's resources and impoverished its people. Dutt showed that the revenue demands under both Permanent Settlement and Ryotwari were at unsustainable levels, and he connected these to the recurring famines. His work was presented to Lord Curzon and is considered one of the foundational texts of Indian economic nationalism. Do not confuse with Dadabhai Naoroji, whose book was 'Poverty and Un-British Rule in India' (1901).
questions.source: SSC CGL — the R.C. Dutt / Naoroji book distinction is a standard trick question; both wrote on economic drain but different books

questions.text: Which of the following correctly explains how British trade policy contributed to the deindustrialisation of India in the 19th century?
options: Britain imposed equal tariffs on both Indian and British goods, but Indian goods were simply of inferior quality

Britain charged high import duties (70-80%) on Indian textiles entering Britain, while charging minimal duties (2.5-5%) on British machine-made goods entering India — protecting British industry while exposing Indian handicrafts
India voluntarily chose to import British goods because they were cheaper, causing Indian weavers to retrain as agricultural workers
The Charter Act of 1833 banned Indian textile exports, forcing weavers into unemployment
questions.explanation: British trade policy was systematically asymmetric: Indian textiles entering Britain faced import duties of 70–80%, effectively barring them from the British market. Meanwhile, British machine-made textiles entering India faced duties of only 2.5–5%. This was not 'free trade' — it was a rigged system where British manufacturing was protected at home while Indian manufacturing was destroyed in its own domestic market by cheap imports. Combined with the Industrial Revolution's technological advantage (machines vs. handlooms), this systematically destroyed India's textile industry. The Charter Act of 1813 (not 1833) ended the Company's monopoly and opened India to British traders, accelerating this process.
questions.source: SSC CGL — the mechanism of deindustrialisation (differential duties) is a frequently tested analytical question

questions.text: The 1876-78 famine that killed millions in South India is associated with which Viceroy, who controversially held a grand Delhi Durbar during the same period?
options: Lord Curzon
Lord Lytton
Lord Chelmsford
Lord Ripon
questions.explanation: The Great Madras Famine of 1876-78 killed an estimated 5-10 million people in Madras and Bombay Presidencies — during Lord Lytton's tenure as Viceroy. At the peak of this famine, Lytton organized the spectacular Delhi Durbar of 1877 to celebrate Queen Victoria's proclamation as Empress of India (Kaisar-i-Hind). The contrast — imperial extravagance while millions starved — was widely condemned. Lytton actually opposed measures to restrict grain exports during the famine, believing market forces should handle distribution. Florence Nightingale and journalist William Digby documented the famine's horrors and criticized the British response.
questions.source: SSC CGL — the 1876-78 famine and Lord Lytton (along with the Delhi Durbar contrast) is a frequently tested fact connecting Viceroys to famines

questions.text: Dadabhai Naoroji was the first Indian elected to the British Parliament. Which constituency did he represent, and in which year?
options: Westminster, 1885
Finsbury (Central London), 1892
Manchester, 1900
Birmingham, 1895
questions.explanation: Dadabhai Naoroji was elected to the British House of Commons in 1892, representing Finsbury Central in London — winning by a majority of just 5 votes. He was the first Indian (and first Asian) MP in the British Parliament. From the floor of Parliament, he raised the issue of the Drain of Wealth, poverty in India, and the need for Indian representation. He served until 1895. His election was celebrated across India as a breakthrough — showing that Indians could participate in British democratic institutions. His opponent in the election was described by Lord Salisbury (Prime Minister) as 'a black man' — a remark that fueled Indian nationalist outrage.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated because 'first Indian MP' is a tested fact but the specific constituency (Finsbury) and year (1892) are detail-level questions in higher-difficulty papers

questions.text: Governor-General Bentinck's 1834 remark about 'bones of cotton-weavers bleaching the plains of India' referred to which specific development?
options: The aftermath of the Vellore Mutiny (1806) which killed many cotton workers
The destruction of Indian handloom weaving by cheap British machine-made textiles flooding Indian markets after the Charter Act 1813
A plague epidemic that particularly affected Bengal's weaving communities in 1832-34
The Indigo Revolt of 1833, in which planters physically attacked cotton weavers who refused to switch crops
questions.explanation: Bentinck's 1834 remark described the catastrophic collapse of India's handloom weaving industry — specifically the Bengal muslin weavers — caused by cheap British machine-made cloth flooding Indian markets after the Charter Act 1813 opened India to all British traders. Dacca muslin, once exported globally and famous for its extraordinary fineness, became commercially unviable when British mills could produce cloth at a fraction of the cost per yard. Bentinck's remark acknowledged the human cost of this deindustrialisation — weavers had no alternative employment and returned to subsistence farming in poverty. The remark is notable because it came from the Governor-General himself, not an Indian critic.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated because the Bentinck quote on weavers is a specific source-based question; the connection to Charter Act 1813 and deindustrialisation is the key context

questions.text: What were 'Home Charges' and why did Indian nationalists consider them a form of economic drain?
options: Charges for building homes for Indian civil servants — resented because only British officers got government housing

Charges levied on Indian taxpayers for the cost of governing India from London — including British officials' salaries/pensions, India Office costs, interest on debt, and military expenses for wars outside India
Import duties charged on goods entering India from Britain — which nationalists saw as too low
Charges paid by Indian zamindars to maintain government guesthouses (dak bungalows) in their districts
questions.explanation: Home Charges were charges that India was required to pay to Britain for the cost of the Indian administration run from London. They included: (1) Salaries and pensions of British civil and military officers (who retired to Britain and were paid from Indian taxes); (2) Interest on debt incurred by the Indian government for railways, public works, and wars — much of this debt was for purchases from Britain; (3) Costs of maintaining the India Office in London — the department that governed India; (4) Military expenditure when British Indian troops were used in wars outside India (e.g., in Afghanistan, China, Africa). Dadabhai Naoroji argued that these charges were a form of tribute — India was paying for its own subjugation. Home Charges were a central component of his calculation of the annual Drain.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated because 'Home Charges' is a specific technical term in economic drain discourse that appears in definition-type questions in higher-difficulty papers

questions.text: The Deccan Riots of 1875 in the Bombay Presidency were caused by which sequence of events?
options: Lord Curzon's revenue enhancement in Deccan district led to farmer protests

The American Civil War (1861-65) created a cotton boom; Indian farmers borrowed heavily to expand cotton; when American supply resumed (1865), prices crashed; farmers lost land to moneylenders; riots followed in 1875
The introduction of Ryotwari System in Deccan caused sudden rent increases, triggering riots
Cholera and drought hit simultaneously in 1874, and farmers rioted against relief camp conditions
questions.explanation: The Deccan Riots (1875) are a textbook example of how global economics could devastate Indian farmers. During the American Civil War (1861-65), US cotton exports to Lancashire stopped — British mills desperately needed Indian cotton. Bombay Deccan farmers were encouraged to expand cotton cultivation; they borrowed heavily from moneylenders to buy land and inputs. When the war ended in 1865 and American cotton resumed, Indian cotton prices crashed. Farmers could not repay debts; moneylenders seized their land and homes. By 1875, desperation turned to violence — farmers attacked moneylender records and burned debt documents. The riots led to the Deccan Agriculturists Relief Act (1879), which gave farmers some legal protection from money lenders.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because Deccan Riots are rarely covered in basic notes; the full American Civil War → cotton boom → debt → riots chain is a specific causal sequence tested in analytical questions

questions.text: The Workmen's Breach of Contract Act (1859) is significant in the history of colonial economic exploitation because:
options: It prevented British companies from firing Indian workers without cause, giving workers job security
It made it a criminal offence (not merely civil) for indentured plantation workers to leave before their contract ended — effectively criminalising their freedom of movement
It allowed Indian workers to sue British employers in Indian courts for unpaid wages
It established the first minimum wage for tea plantation workers in Assam
questions.explanation: The Workmen's Breach of Contract Act (1859) was one of the most exploitative pieces of colonial legislation. Indentured labor worked on British-owned tea plantations in Assam and Darjeeling under contracts. If a worker left before the contract ended (even to escape abuse or seek better wages), it was treated as a criminal offence — the worker could be arrested and prosecuted. Under ordinary (civil) contract law, breach would only lead to a civil lawsuit for damages. Criminalising it meant the power of the police and criminal courts was used to trap workers in plantation conditions. The Act was a form of bonded labour enforced by the state. It was repealed in 1926 under nationalist pressure.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because the Workmen's Breach of Contract Act is rarely taught in school-level Modern History; the 'criminal vs civil' distinction is the key exam point

questions.text: M.G. Ranade's contribution to Indian economic nationalism differed from Dadabhai Naoroji's primarily in that Ranade:
options: Focused entirely on quantifying the drain and never proposed solutions
Focused on advocating positive industrialisation and protection of Indian industries — not just criticising British drain but arguing India needed its own industrial development
Argued that Indian poverty was caused by caste system and not British policies
Opposed Swadeshi — he believed Indian goods were inferior and should not be promoted
questions.explanation: Mahadev Govind Ranade (1842–1901), a judge and economist from Bombay, took a constructive approach to economic nationalism. While Naoroji and R.C. Dutt focused on documenting the drain, Ranade argued that India needed to build its own industrial base — not just protest British exploitation. In his 'Essays on Indian Economics' (1898), he argued for: protection of Indian 'infant industries' from foreign competition, encouragement of Indian entrepreneurship, development of technical education, and state support for industrialisation. His ideas influenced the later industrial vision of the Congress and foreshadowed post-independence policies like import substitution and public sector development. He is considered the father of Indian economic nationalism in its positive/constructive sense.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because Ranade is a 'minor figure' in SSC notes but his distinction from Naoroji (constructive vs. critical) is tested in analytical questions

questions.text: Which of the following correctly matches the economist/nationalist with their major work on British economic exploitation of India?
options: Dadabhai Naoroji — 'Economic History of India'; R.C. Dutt — 'Poverty and Un-British Rule in India'
Dadabhai Naoroji — 'Poverty and Un-British Rule in India' (1901); R.C. Dutt — 'Economic History of India' (1902)
Bal Gangadhar Tilak — 'Economic History of India'; M.G. Ranade — 'Poverty and Un-British Rule'
G.K. Gokhale — 'Poverty and Un-British Rule'; Dadabhai Naoroji — 'Essays on Indian Economics'
questions.explanation: The correct match: Dadabhai Naoroji wrote 'Poverty and Un-British Rule in India' (1901) — the book that named and quantified the Drain of Wealth. Romesh Chandra Dutt (R.C. Dutt) wrote 'Economic History of India' (2 vols, 1902-04) — statistical documentation of how British policy impoverished India. This is one of the most common 'swap the books' trick questions in SSC CGL Modern History. M.G. Ranade wrote 'Essays on Indian Economics' (1898). The Naoroji-Dutt book swap is the specific trap — memorise: Naoroji = Poverty/Un-British Rule; Dutt = Economic History.
questions.source: Naoroji/Dutt book matching is the most frequently tested 'match the book to the author' question in Economic Impact of British Rule — appears in multiple SSC CGL years

questions.text: The process by which India was transformed from a textile exporter to a raw material exporter for British factories is called:
options: Commercialisation of agriculture
Economic drain
Deindustrialisation
The Home Charges mechanism
questions.explanation: Deindustrialisation refers specifically to the decline and destruction of Indian manufacturing and handicraft industries under British rule — particularly textiles. India went from being a major exporter of finished textiles (Dacca muslin, calico, chintz) to exporting raw cotton and jute (raw materials) and importing manufactured textiles from Britain. This reversal was caused by differential trade duties (protecting British manufacturers while exposing Indian weavers to competition) and the technological advantage of British steam-powered mills. Commercialisation of agriculture (farmers shifting to cash crops) and Economic Drain (wealth leaving India) are related but different processes. Deindustrialisation specifically refers to the destruction of manufacturing.
questions.source: The term 'deindustrialisation' and its definition is a standard definition/identification question in SSC CGL Modern History

questions.text: The 1943 Bengal Famine, which killed an estimated 2-3 million people, is considered 'man-made' rather than a natural disaster primarily because:
options: There was no drought in 1943 — food production was adequate, but wartime policies diverted grain, blocked relief, and exported food while millions starved
The famine was deliberately engineered by Subhas Chandra Bose's INA to destabilise British control of Bengal
A fungal disease destroyed the Bengal rice crop completely — purely a natural agricultural disaster
The partition of Bengal (reversed in 1911) had permanently damaged Bengal's agricultural infrastructure
questions.explanation: The 1943 Bengal Famine is described as 'man-made' by economists including Amartya Sen (whose Nobel Prize-winning work analysed famines). Key facts: (1) The 1943 rice harvest was not dramatically below normal — there was no massive crop failure; (2) Churchill's wartime policies prioritised food for troops and Britain; grain exports from India continued even as Bengal starved; (3) The 'scorched earth' policy in coastal Bengal (destroying boats and rice stocks to prevent Japanese use) removed food and transport; (4) The Bengal government and Viceroy Linlithgow were slow to acknowledge and respond to the crisis; (5) Churchill reportedly dismissed Indian requests for food relief. Sen's theory: famines occur not from food shortage alone but from breakdown of 'entitlements' — people's ability to acquire food. In 1943, Bengal had food but poor people couldn't buy it as prices spiralled.
questions.source: The man-made nature of the 1943 Bengal Famine and Amartya Sen's analysis is a frequently tested fact in economic history questions; also appears in Science-Economics overlap sections
